title: vimeo_1201958213
displayTitle: 2026-06-17 - Health Committee - 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Health
description: Wednesday 17 June 2026 | Health Committee 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Health Hearing of Evidence: Associate Minister of Health (Pharmac) Hon David Seymour Pharmaceutical Management Agency Paula Bennett, Board Chair Natalie McMurtry, Chief Executive Minister for Seniors and Associate Minister of Health Hon Casey Costello Health New Zealand—Te Whatu Ora Dr Dale Bramley, Chief Executive Jason Power, Acting Executive National Director - Planning, Funding and Outcomes Selah Hart, Interim National Director Hauora Māori Service Ministry of Health Audrey Sonerson, Director-General of Health Ruth Issac, Deputy Director-General, Strategy and Policy Group Andrew Old, Deputy Director-General, Public Health Agency and Mental Health Group
descriptionSummary: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Health. Associate Minister of Health (Pharmac). Hon David Seymour

chunks.text: Simple.

Welcome, Minister Seymour, Honorable Paula Bennett, Mrs. McMurtrie, very good to have you here. We've got you in here for half an hour. I'll leave it to you to make some short opening remarks, and then we'll go to questions. Thank you. Yes. Well, look, thank you to the chair and the committee for this opportunity to share all the wonderful news about what has been achieved in Pharmac and also Medsafe over the last year, and indeed, the last two and a half years, where enormous progress has been made in the functioning of the organizations and the service that's received by New

Zealanders. I think it's fair to say that two and a half years ago, we inherited Pharmac in a poor state. It did not have certainty of funding. There was a war of words between the chief executive of Pharmac and various stakeholders who were protesting outside the building.

There was very slow performance in assessing medicines despite low volumes of medicines being assessed. An enormous amount of frustration in the entire sector. Today, we have a new chair, a new chief executive, a program of improvement within the culture of Pharmac. We have seen eight new medicines funded and 28 with widened access in the '25, '26 year, following 31 new listings and 52 with widened access in the previous year.

Altogether, 360,000 patients have benefited from this new access. We've also set about new ways of thinking about funding Pharmac as we transform the organization from economizing within a fixed budget to becoming an advocate for a larger pharmaceutical budget where savings can be made elsewhere.

And I'm happy to talk about that ongoing project. In the case of Medsafe, we've roughly halved the time for consents depending on their complexity, and we've introduced a new initiative, being the rule of two, where if a medicine is approved or consented in the last four years in any of a number of other jurisdictions, then it will be consented in New Zealand within 30 days of application. This is a major change that will bring medicines access, frankly, to where it should've been for a long time, up with the rest of the world.

So those are just some of the things that have occurred. I'm sure there'll be people that want to ask more specific questions. Very happy to share more of the good news. Thank you, Minister. Chloe Swarbrick, primary. And then Ashleigh. Kia ora. I just wanted to ask about medical devices. So I've got a few different questions just to try and actually explore what's going on here.

Firstly, can you confirm whether Pharmac runs evergreen contracts on medical devices? Evergreen contracts? Yeah. What do you mean by that? Effectively, contracts that auto-renew and are changed only by mutual consent between Pharmac and the medical device company. Well, if you want to ask about the specifics of contracts, I'll refer you to the chief executive.

Okay. We do have a number of contracts that have evergreen but checkpoints for renewal. And that helps us keep prices stable over time. And we use a mix of some are and some aren't, but we do have those. And we have those in pharmaceuticals as well on occasion, it helps us stabilize prices over the longer term.

Really keen to pick that point up. But just to further explore this evergreen point. So when was the last competitive process run for medical devices? We just ran one right before Christmas on drug-eluting stents. Mm-hmm. And yeah. Okay. So I understand that, rather, is Pharmac aware of any current contracted arrangements that are offering hospitals a lower price in exchange for a written commitment to buy less than 10% of their volume from competing suppliers?

I am not aware. You're not aware of those? Okay. My understanding is that there have been some smaller players that have written to you to that effect, so I can follow up with some further questions. Are you aware of Christchurch New Zealand, the economic development arm of Christchurch City Council, having run some surveys of medical health tech companies who have said at least half of them found it impossible to navigate the health system procurement processes?

We're not aware of that correspondence. Nonetheless, we frequently engage with people who are frustrated by not having their product supported or feel that the procedures are more bureaucratic than they'd need to be. That's why we have, after 12 years of fighting between Health New Zealand and Pharmac, put in place a fair division of who procures which types of devices. And as a result, the two organizations are now able to streamline their processes.

But I'm not surprised that people would be frustrated with what they've experienced over that period. And I guess, Minister, to that earlier point of the confirmation that there are evergreen contracts that exist for medical devices, that kind of contradicts what I understand was your letter that you wrote along with Simeon Brown to require that the procurement pipeline is refreshed annually. Does it surprise you to hear that there are evergreen contracts for medical devices?

Well, not really, because in any kind of arrangement, you have to decide how much you're going to spend on the transactions, how much bureaucracy you want versus how much you can save through that contestability. So there's certainly a view that there should be markets for everything and every arrangement at all times should be up for reset.

Other times, there are people who say, "No, we reject that neoliberal outlook. We would like to see a little bit more certainty over time, and you can be more efficient and get more value for it." Mm-hmm. That's an ongoing debate amongst a variety of people. I'm interested to see the side the member's taken. So just further to explore particularly this point, though, Pharmac, in correspondence that I've seen, more than happy to share.

Pharmac has said that it will only reprioritize procurement for medical devices where savings exceed about a million dollars a year. Is that an official Pharmac position? Well, again, it's up to the chief executive to decide these sorts of criteria. Yeah, totally. Whoever's most appropriate to answer it. It's all good.

I think we're currently going through the transition of separating the work between Pharmac and Health New Zealand. And one of the big pieces of work there is to figure out a way to prioritize which areas we want to spend the time jointly procuring and going through the procurement processes. So, we do look at large number of contracts and try to prioritize where we think the most savings will be by doing a large procurement process that is joint between us, and we're relying on Health New Zealand now in the new model of trying

to prioritize together which ones we want to work on. I guess further to that point in particular, I've seen correspondence, and I'm interested as to whether it's come across your guys' desk, that in one specific documented case, Pharmac has gone back to one of these small medical device suppliers or small businesses and said that a saving that Pharmac had agreed would constitute about $300,000 a year was too small to pursue. Is that something that concerns you guys?

Well, not necessarily, because Pharmac, like any organization, has limited time. Mm-hmm. A lot of contracts, and it has to decide which ones to do first. I think if we were to discover that they had a choice of pursuing a contract that would save a million dollars or pursuing a contract that would save $300,000, obviously you'd want them to do both.

But if they had to choose, you'd want them to do the one that saved a million dollars. So it wouldn't be surprising to hear something like that if you start to think through the logic of what an organization like Pharmac is trying to achieve. Nonetheless, as the chief executive has indicated, we are in the process of a transition after basically Simeon and Brown and I negotiating a peace after a civil war between Pharmac and Health New Zealand that went on for a dozen years.

And as they work through their new systems, I'm sure that they'll be able to get to the savings for smaller amounts as well as larger ones. Just to be clear, in this specific instance, it wasn't a trade-off between a saving of 300K and a million. It was just completely shutting down the potential of saving around $300,000. Well, you don't know that because it's possible that one vendor has been told that they couldn't prioritize their transaction at that time. Pharmac might have had reasons for not pursuing it that

they haven't revealed to you or the person who's given you their email. Okay, so further to that point then, can I get a commitment from yourself, Minister, or from Pharmac that you'd be willing to look into specifically this case to understand it as a test case of the issues that are being faced by- Yeah, absolutely. If we've got all the facts, yeah. Can I just ask, have you previously raised this as a constituency MP or in any other capacity?

It's not in my constituency, but I understand it's been raised directly with all three of you. Okay. Well, if you can- Including in direct meetings. If you can- And I think there's a number of factors around it. So it would be best that we took that off. Yeah, so I'm not going to make you any obligations right now without knowing all of the facts and knowing exactly where that sits at this point. Right. Because as you say, I do know who it probably is, and we've certainly had meetings in relatively recent times.

Exactly. What I'd suggest is if you've had an issue like this before, again, then you can come to us anytime. Probably easier to work out something at this level of detail constructively rather than to raise here, but up to you. Again, I think I've proven with you, David, that I'm very much willing to do that constructively. I'll go to Aisha Vera.

Go straight with her question. Thank you. Thanks, Mr. Chair. Minister, an opportunity to update us on how your priorities are evolving in the year ahead. You came to office, and previously told the committee you wanted Pharmac to improve the funding bids that it makes.

You gave the example of whole of society benefits for funding drugs for multiple sclerosis, diabetes, and migraines. Why hasn't that type of work been visible yet? It's a very good question, and I think it's worthy of discussion. I would summarize that there's two basic frustrations, which I've shared in the media. One is that in order to demonstrate that there is a saving elsewhere, you need to actually know what that saving is.

And what we've discovered is that while Pharmac is incredibly fastidious about its costs and prioritization and so on, the rest of the healthcare system finds it difficult to show what the costs of their services are, and therefore, what savings might arise if the investment in a new medicine was to displace that treatment or that procedure, and so on. So that's the first challenge that we're facing.

The second is to do with timing. So if you take, for example, there's been a lot of discussion about can GLP-1s reduce obesity. It appears they can. Can that reduce diabetes? I'm sure it can. Can that reduce kidney failure? Can that reduce dialysis? Well, all of those things are true, but the timeframe for that sequence of events to happen is nearly a decade. So for a government that is trying to justify a budget uplift in 2026, 2027, to say that this may have savings in 2035, for example, it doesn't work well with the way that successive governments have budgeted.

So those are the two roadblocks that we've run into, but we remain resolute that we can fix them. Supplementary. Minister, I put it to you that if you open a textbook on cost benefit analysis, those limitations would've been in it before the election. This was part of your party's election commitment. Why haven't you been able to deliver operationalizing this model in three years?

I guess because we like to try hard things. So yep, you could've said, "This is too hard, we're just not gonna try." But we think- You can fix those hard things... which is what we are doing. I think if you were to say that we shouldn't have tried, we should've stayed with the model that we had, remained one of the lowest funded countries in the world for medicines, then yeah, that was an option. It's not an option that I would accept.

Sometimes hard things are worth doing because they're difficult. Okay. But they haven't been done yet. Supplementary- Yeah, and we've been quite open about that... the ACT Party's policy was for a medicine strategy. I'm aware you've received briefings on a medicine strategy. In the coming year, will a medicine strategy be delivered?

The answer is no. And can I just say that I love talking about the ACT Party, but I know if I do, you'll probably say that I shouldn't be talking about politics in a strict hearing like this. Why won't a medicine strategy be delivered when it was part of the direction you set as minister at the beginning of your term?

Because we decided that given the range of challenges that we face, if you look at what we've done, we've absorbed the device issue, we've changed the culture, we've changed the management, the chair, the leadership. We've made unending changes that have been for the benefit of patients in New Zealand. We decided that amongst all of those, writing another document wasn't necessary.

Two years ago here, I asked you about your stated plans for partnerships, and you indicated that those partnerships might be partial government funding for a drug under investigation. Have any partnerships been approved by Pharmac or are there any in the works that mean that that initial direction you set will be acted on in the coming year?

Well, given it takes you into the nature of contracts, I'll let the chief executive answer if that's okay. Could you just repeat the context? Before your tenure at Pharmac- Mm... there was an indication that Pharmac would be entering into partnerships with the private sector.

For example, a drug that was still under investigation perhaps would be given partial funding in order to gain access in New Zealand. Are any of those types of partnerships likely to be agreed in the year ahead? We don't have any that are at a point where they would be agreeing.

We are definitely looking at international best practice and what some other countries are doing with those emerging drugs and if it can come sooner. But we don't have anything that we'd be planning to in this year that I'm aware of yet. I'd have to go back to the team for more information. Thanks. Minister, these are three big items about how Pharmac does its work that it appears you're telling the committee there's been no progress on and we can't expect more progress in the year ahead.

A couple of things about that. Number one, I don't consider a specific type of contracting as a big item when you've just contracted new access to medicines that's given 360,000 people more access to medicine. So let's talk a little bit about the relative size here. I wouldn't say that was big. Similarly, writing a document or a strategy, I don't consider that big.

What is big is all of the other things that we've done- Well, the ACT Party leader might disagree with you because it was his medicines, his policy. Let the minister answer. Yeah, fair enough. But if I was to... I'd love to. Can I be the ACT Party leader for a moment? So if I was to channel him, I think he would say, very, very proud of what the ACT Party has achieved through the medicines portfolio. It's the access to medicines.

It's the change in culture. It's the work on the whole of society methodology. All of these things are huge. Did we get a medicine strategy and some partnership contracts? No. But overwhelmingly great news, and we're very happy with the way it's gone. Carlos, primary. Thank you. Thank you, Chair. You mentioned the rule of two just now.

Mm. I just want to know how many medicine have been approved today through the rule of two pathway. We haven't done any through the rule of two yet because that will only come into effect next month. Mm. The legislation, as I'm sure you're aware, was passed near the end of last year. Great. We've recently done some of the final implementation details such as setting the fees, which will be cheaper than Australia, significantly cheaper than Australia, to consent a medicine, which is great news.

And then as of early next month they'll be able to take the first applications under the rule of two. Thank you. Jenny, primary. Oh, thank you very much. Thank you, Minister. Thank you for being here today. One of the things that we heard previously was in terms of geopolitics and the impacts that tariffs from America could impact on the supply of medicines and the cost of medicines coming into New Zealand.

Has any of that played out in real time and are there any concerns that you hold that that could still be an ongoing effect as we move into this next financial year? The short answer is that we've managed it very well. It hasn't materially affected New Zealand's access to medicines. It may be that- One of my colleagues has more to say about it. Yeah, we've been really lucky in that we haven't seen supply issues because of the unrest in the Middle East. But we, and the chief executive can speak to it, we've constantly got supply

issues. So at any one time, we've got about 100 medicines that we might be looking at that for whatever reasons, could be a massive uptake in need globally, it could be that they're changing brand, it could be in manufacturing. So there's all sorts of stuff that's constantly going on.

In my opinion, no one within government does it better than Pharmac as far as they're constantly looking ahead as to what's happening, brand changes, what the options are and things like that. I think one of the concerns that certainly the chief executive and the senior leadership team have raised with the board is perhaps an increase in costs because of freight costs, because of manufacturing and things like that.

So we're still getting the access that she might add too, but it is more there could be an increase in cost that we're trying to mitigate when we look at our out-year funding and how we do that. Yeah. The only thing I would add is that in this budget that we've just had, we have added $13.5 million. Now, that money, in the first instance, can be used to absorb such costs, although if any suppliers are watching, you didn't hear that.

However, I hope that managing those costs will allow us to actually invest in more medicines, and also streamlines and upgrades some of the operations so we can process medicines faster through Pharmac. Thank you. Just on that, in terms of this year's budget uplift, good to see New Zealand First national coalition agreement was to increase the funding for Pharmac on a regular basis.

There has been a considerable amount over the last three years of finance, more money going into purchase medicines. You mentioned the 360,000 patients that now are receiving medicines. What do you expect this budget uplift, what will the impact be on patients? And what needs to happen next, getting more medicines off the OFI list? What is the trajectory moving forward?

Oh, gee, I didn't realize you had that commitment. You should've helped me argue for even more money. Yeah. Could've been helpful. Damn it. I did drop you a note, though. What an opportunity lost. Look, in terms of the $13.5 million, we hope that that will be able to help as many as 50,000 people get additional access in some way. But as I've said, that's contingent on price uplifts could be potentially a problem.

And also the fact that we've just got to work through a difficult year for the whole world before we can be sure about that impact. And to give you an indication in, I think, as to how well Pharmac do, as the minister identified in his opening remarks. In the last financial year with no uplift in funding, effectively we had 36 either widening or new medicines come on board affecting that many people.

So a big part of, which is interesting even with the medical devices questions that we were getting straight off. All we want to do is procure and renegotiate to try and bring down prices so that we can then invest that in other medicines. So what we can potentially do with that 13 and a half is hopefully it's 50,000. But equally, we can prove what we can do just by renegotiating, making savings, bundling things better, getting better at our processes as to how we recover, that we then reinvest that obviously into what's as high as we can on the

OFI. But our challenge going forward, which I know you identify as emerging technologies and emerging medicines, and when you think about gene therapy and people's expectations of what they should be able to get, and they should. It's kind of mind-boggling that in this day and age, we can give the kind of treatments that don't actually make people sicker to then try and get better.

Literally, as we can see with the Malaghan Institute and CAR T therapy and things like that. But man, are they expensive. Really expensive. So as a country, and that's the challenges as we move forward, as to how you can afford these amazing new medicines, technology, GLPs. They're fantastic. They're very expensive in that context.

Thank you. Dr. Ardern. Thank you, Mr. Chair. These questions are all related to the theme of medicines. Pharmac ran a consultation on removing items- You decide politics and decisions. Pharmac ran a consultation on removing items from the Options for Investment list. Will that work continue in the year under discussion?

And if not, why not? I'll refer that to the chief executive. I try not to get involved in their specific decisions. Thank you for the question. Yes, we had quite a lot of feedback from our patient family working group that we have as part of the reset, as well as other consumer groups over time and suppliers, that they felt they would like more certainty with knowing things that are lower ranked on the OFI or potentially wouldn't be ranked.

And so we went out to consultation publicly on that question. Should we remove some of the lower-ranked medicines on the OFI that have a smaller chance of being funded with the knowledge that you could come back and reapply or re-request? And we heard resoundingly, "No, actually. We think we don't really want that. We want to leave things on the list," and the hope that they would be funded and that they would remain eligible for funding.

And that's what we've done. So we don't have any intention to change that. It was really interesting because we thought people were saying we'd rather know and have the certainty, as Natalie said. But actually, once you went out there and consulted, and I met with a few of them, and they'd go, "Well, actually, that medicine's now so old, and it's been superseded by a far more modern one, and so why don't we take off the old one and then we'll re..." And then he said, it was a specialist, and he said, "And then I thought about it, and I thought, well, I'd rather keep that one and have something

that's a maybe or a hope than nothing." We did it for the right intentions, but then we listened to the consultation. So you've heard that people would rather have a hope of having the item funded, even though we know from the statistics items can spend an awful long time on the options for investment list.

Some have, yeah. Very long time. Seven years is the average, right? And that's because some are extraordinary and as I think the chief executive was being careful, but the likelihood is quite slim for some. That's true, but also I'd just point, the number that have come off and been funded in the last few years is greater than at any time in a decade, two decades. We've had an enormous amount of cash put into Pharmac, and therefore new medicines being funded. So perhaps we live in more hopeful times than previously.

And Mr. Chair, also keeping with the theme of access to medicines, I know of the government's recent points about medical lockers. What work is occurring within government to make sure that the implementation of medical lockers, where drugs can be picked up by a person from a locker if they can't make it to the pharmacy during hours, that the implementation of that is safe and people get the appropriate advice about their medicine when they picked it up?

Ultimately, that's going to be a professional judgment. So if you are a pharmacist, you have a whole lot of professional obligations in terms of what's the ultimate outcome of people receiving medicines that you dispense. So you would expect that we can actually trust professionals. I don't believe that people that spend their whole life and are passionate about their profession should be cocooned in rules that just make life harder for no reason.

I choose to trust professionals. See if that applies to all government policy. Supplementary. Indeed, so do I. Even the comedians- So-... and the little ones... is there any discussion with those professionals about how to make this change workable for them with those safety rules in mind?

Well, it's pharmacists that are asking for it. That's the first thing. Second of all, the way that it will be implemented will be through a change to the Medicines Act, which is currently being worked on by Casey Costello. So you can imagine all of the usual lawmaking procedures that go on. There will be consultation on the bill. It will go off to a select committee.

I'm sure that if anybody can see a gaping hole or problem with it, then those lawmaking procedures that New Zealanders rely on will come to their aid. Charles, primary. Thank you, Minister. I just want to talk about your last week announcement, talking about pharmacists going to be responsible for the funding for ambulance medicine used by the ambulance.

Can you give us a little bit more insight in how this will affect the budget for budget 2026? Yeah. Obviously there's a cost to that, but at the same time, there are other agencies that were previously paying, so that will now be funded through Pharmac, and I think it makes sense to have the organization that is just really good at assessing and buying medicines doing that for the ambulance service.

Yeah. So that's one thing I don't get from your announcement is you believe that the medicine will be cheaper because they changed the funding model from Health New Zealand and ACC to Pharmac. So how does that work? Well, we're just better. Yeah, a little bit. But no, in all seriousness, Pharmac buys $1.8 billion worth of medicine every year.

So when someone comes along and says, "We buy $1.8 billion," you tend to get a better deal than if you're St. John or whoever, much respect as we have for them. And they were sort of buying across the board, and it was not as keen, and we just saw that we had the expertise that we could bring in. There is some cost to Pharmac, but in the scheme of it, fairly minimal when it comes to the advantages that we can give to make sure. And it's the small stocks that they need, as you can imagine, within ambulances working with the hospitals because when they do need to

restock sometimes that's where it is. So we can streamline that for it. It actually makes it cheaper for our ambulance services. But equally, and we've also widened the access that they're able to give a medicine within the ambulance that they couldn't before. So I think that stuff all makes a difference. And as a board and giving some direction, we're quite excited about other areas. There are other areas where they hold medicines and how they access them and at what cost.

Actually, there's medical outlets in some of our corrections facilities, for example. It's sort of looking across the board as to how we might be able to, looking at some of our doctors' surgeries, particularly in rural areas, as to what they might actually keep on site in smaller quantities if that's going to help access for people, which is the whole aim of it at the end of the day.

Thank you. We're on time. Is that it? I've got two more questions for you if you have them. Yeah. Yeah. Sweet. Go for it. Lucky last. So yeah. Last two- Yeah... just very briefly. You get one. You get one. Appreciate the commitment to working constructively to try and resolve these issues that have been raised- Yeah, just notice now... and of course these have been raised in the past.

But nonetheless, just two quick questions. The first is, are you aware, and this is perhaps better directed to Pharmac, how many people currently work within Pharmac on medical device procurement? I'd have to come back with the exact number. Okay. My understanding is about 16. Does that sound about- That sounds close. Okay. The second, and this is potentially for the minister and/or...

Well, is really for the minister. Just to your point earlier, Minister Seymour, about brokering the peace between Te Whatu Ora, Health New Zealand, and Pharmac. You'll probably be aware that in 2022 there was a review which came to the conclusion that, actually, the procurement processes for medical devices perhaps best sat within Health New Zealand.

Why do you disagree with that position? Because there's different types of devices. So there are some that are very adjacent to the clinician, and if it's something that they need to buy in large volume, where basically they're going to be a huge number of, I don't know, gloves or perhaps a bed or a device that clinicians use, then the people that employ the clinicians should be involved in that, and the people that are closest to using them.

On the other hand, there are other types of devices where the costs and benefits are open to scrutiny, and the organization that has the best ability to evaluate costs and benefits is Pharmac. And so as a result, we now have, I think if memory serves, about 56 different categories of medical devices. Roughly half of those now sit with Health New Zealand.

Roughly half now sit with Pharmac. And they've been allocated according to whether they are more of a bulk volume, close to clinician Health New Zealand thing, or more of a technical, complex benefit, require tough cost benefit analysis, and therefore more of a Pharmac thing. All right. Thank you, Minister. Thank you, Chair.
Thank you. Thanks. Thank you.
Oh, good. Well,
there.

Welcome, Minister Costello, Chief Executive Dale Bramley, Stella Hart, everybody else involved. Very good to have you here. We have you here for 30 minutes, Minister, so there's time for some brief introductory remarks, and then we'll hand it over to questions from members.

Thank you. Thank you, Mr. Chair, and to the committee. I would like to touch on just a couple of points around the Budget 2026 portfolio areas that I hold. In my emergency services responsibilities, 35 million over four years has been invested to strengthen road ambulance services. This investment is to deliver immediate improvements for patients and frontline staff in meeting growing demand.

It supports two ambulance hubs to be established in Auckland, with one confirmed in South Auckland. It also supports an electronic patient clinical record system, additional training support for ambulance communication center staff, and additional clinical welfare checks for patients. This budget investment is in addition to a funding increase from Health New Zealand and ACC, which is being negotiated for road ambulances to meet demand and cost pressures.

It also sits alongside investments in stronger air ambulance services. Seven helicopters are already in service, and nine are scheduled to enter into service between now and mid-2027. I want to turn now to maternal health. Budget 2026 provides 34.4 million over four years to support mothers and babies in their critical first days after birth. We're investing in the resourcing needed to enable mothers to choose to receive up to three days of inpatient postnatal care after giving birth.

This supports our government's focus on giving babies the best possible start in life and providing mothers with choice over how and where they receive support after giving birth. This funding will increase inpatient postnatal care bed capacity and the workforce and support implementation of the Three Day Postnatal Stay Amendment Bill, which Minister Brown is responsible for.

Finally, in regard to aged care, you'll be aware that the last year the government established an independent aged care ministerial advisory group to provide expert advice on long-term reform of the aged care system. We're expecting the group's report in the coming weeks, which will help inform future decisions. In the interim, the government continues to make investments to sustain services and ensure older people can access timely care. The latest 4.C in funding uplift announced this morning will provide the aged residential care

sector with an extra $79 million this year. Thank you, and happy to take questions. Thank you, Minister. Dr. Barrett. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you, Minister. Can you explain your claim that the St John's Ambulance now receives 90% of its operational funding from the government?

So, the funding received is a mix of ACC and health funding, plus the co-payment. So where I've been quoted in that is including the co-payment portion of that, so in terms of the funding that they receive. So that is differentiating what is there. Is it correct that St John's receives 90% of its operational funding from government sources?

So I'm not sure what specifically you're quoting. The references that I have made around funding is to do with the funding they receive from ACC, from Health New Zealand, and the co-payment. And is it your view that that adds up to 90%? I'd have to check the figures. I'm not sure if anyone can provide me the exact numbers around I don't probably have them with me, but we could find them.

Yeah, we could get the specific numbers. Yeah, it's 93% government funding plus part charges. Yeah. Plus- 93%. Plus part charges. So how much is government funding without the part charges? I'm sorry, I'd have to go back. In terms of percentage of their cost, and this is where it's difficult to work through that funding ratio because it is reliant on what the ambulance services themselves have as operating costs.

So it does vary. So based on this information from officials, and New Zealanders know that the cost of an ambulance has gone up from approximately $100 to $135 under this government, is it correct that the government is providing 90% of St John's funding? What I can talk specifically to what we fund in terms of the amount, but the percentage of their operating costs, I'd have to come back and clarify that.

Why were you certain when you spoke to Radio New Zealand that well over 90% of St John's funding was provided by government on the 22nd of May? I can't refer to the specific quote. What the information I was relying on- It's from you... was as ... Yeah, I don't know the interview, so I'd have to go back and refer to the specifics of that comment.

But what I was referring to was the official advice that says 93% is now covered by co-payments and the contribution from ACC and Health New Zealand. Before this budget, St John's indicated to me they would need an extra 145 million per year in order to be 90% funded by government. How can the 8.75 million per year in this budget possibly bridge that gap?

I'm sorry that you're quoting the St John's commentary. What I am saying is what the official advice has been around what their costs are and what we have been funding. So, their current contract negotiations are in play, and then the additional funding was separate to their contract.

Is it the government's position that St John's should receive 90% of its funding from the government? No, that's not the government's position. That's what the status was, that 93% from ACC, Health New Zealand, and co-payment from their operating cost was the figures that we were confirmed was being provided.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. What do you think the funding level from government for St John's should be? I think that we should fund as far as possible within the government constraints. But that is part of negotiation. I'm not going to state a government position. That is, we want to ensure that the services are provided and that they are delivering quality of service, and this is what we're investing in technology, into staffing, into training.

Have you asked the New Zealand First party what their position is on this issue? Well, we have a coalition agreement commitment around funding for New Zealand for ambulance services. And do you believe it's been met? I believe we have increased the funding for ambulance services as per our coalition agreement.

Linda Palmer. Thank you, Mr. Chair, and kia ora, Minister. I wanted to explore with you around vaping and the growth of vaping in our population, and how from a cessation perspective, the infrastructure that we have is built around quit smoking, and hearing from community around how to access vape to quit services are limited.

Wanting to understand what is the process in terms of how we can speed up access for those who you smoke, you access the vape support to quit. Then it is an issue now where there are people just going straight to vapes, and so there isn't NRT necessarily approved for the quit to vape services. Is there any development going on right now in terms of the way that we can improve access, understanding that Pharmac hasn't met with Medsafe yet-... to be able to fund the NRT for quit to vapes?

Yeah. The access of other products. So I think the important part is that this is about the nicotine addiction component. So yes, Health New Zealand is definitely open to working through programs to support the quit vape activity, and I met with all of the quit smoking providers over the last two or three weeks to talk about those initiatives. We are still focused on getting those smokers to stop. It has been encouraging when we look at the data, particularly from ASH, and in terms of the vape sales that are occurring, that vaping is reducing.

That has been encouraging. I think the data we have around The estimated sales for vapes in 2024 was 280 million. In 2022, it was 404 million. So we have seen a reduction in vape sales, and ASH's survey is also recognizing that vaping numbers have been decreasing over the last three years. But yes, it is an important conversation that we're providing supports. Some of the quit smoking providers are already doing that and providing some support in that space.

But having a dedicated program will be the next phase of the- Yeah, there was the Vape to Quit initiative that was launched last year, and it was specifically for face-to-face support, which is awesome. There are more and more whanau that would seek to access Vape to Quit support online or digitally.

Is there any provision to support other providers like the tried and proven Quitline to come on board with that service? Yeah, I think the caution retail in that is that we want to ensure that they are engaged with a quit smoking support. How that support is provided, I think there is some flexibility around how we can do that without having to have face-to-face meetings. But we did want to make sure that it is part of a quit smoking program, that we're not just supplying vapes. So, I think the way those services are delivered,

and that was part of meeting with the quit smoking providers, was to talk through how can we expand that capacity and capability. It has been really successful in terms of the high number of quits from those that are CO verified quits in that space through that program. So I think expanding that may be useful. But again, we want to make sure they are actually engaged with services to ensure that they are being CO verified, and that that isn't just a way of getting dual access to the system. So I think there is some work we can do in that space.

Just to build on that, thinking of a particular population that is vulnerable and has high smoking but also vaping uptake. Tangata Whaiora have spoken about how can there be some focus around support into the mental health community for bespoke services to support them to vape.

Yeah. So we've gone from tobacco. It's now the issue around vapes, how to support these vulnerable populations for quit. Absolutely, and I think that's part of targeting those that we know are high smoking users. There have been some successful engagements with correction services, so ensuring that we're capturing people as they're leaving the corrections environment to be supported to continue to not smoke. But I think all of those initiatives, in some areas it's kind of being a little bit more bold about what level of support we can provide.

But that was definitely part of the conversations around accessing those that have the highest need. And this will be a final question on this line to allow for other supps. It's my understanding that services who operate in the quit smoking realm, if they don't carry the vapes prescribed or some service contracts, that they will no longer be delivering smoking cessation services.

Yeah, so that is part of the package or the suite of options, because if we are saying that these are the tools and options available for a quit smoking service, then all of our quit smoking services need to be providing those options. And so we could, in essence, see some long-standing smoking cessation providers lose contracts and loss of that institutional knowledge.

Yeah, and this is the process that we are dealing with, a changing environment in terms of those smoking needs. So we need to ensure that our providers are delivering the services, and all of this is performance managed around how many quits they are achieving, what is the engagement they're having, what is the success of the program. So I think it's reasonable to have a performance monitoring process within those provider relationships.

Do you think it makes the provider and the- I have a motion to Marsha Vero here. Thank you. Thanks, Mr. Chair. What evidence does this vape kits program have that the quits are in addition to the number of people quitting that was being achieved before the vape kits were added?

I'd have to get specific data, but I think the numbers that we had around the Vape to Quit program was, just checking the numbers. So during the January to July 2025 pilot, participating providers supplied kits to 2,150 clients. 79% of the clients who received a vape kit achieved a CO validated quit, accounting for 37% of all the CO validated quits recorded by participating providers during that period.

So it equated to about 37% of the total quits. Yeah. Thank you. I'm aware you have a very well-qualified public health physician sitting on your left, and I wonder if he might want to take a go at my question, which is about quits in addition to the people who were quitting before the vaping was introduced to this program.

So the standard rate once you're referred to stop smoking services, validation after a period is around 60% to 70%. So for those who got offered the vaping kit, the rate of quitting and validating of that is actually slightly higher to the 70% to 80% range.
Thank you. And when will this evaluation be published?

I don't have that information. I have the- We're currently just working in partnership with the Ministry to establish a regulatory process. Well, it'll be good to see that happen. Carlos. So just talking about helping people to quit smoking, do you think that it will be great if we can put on some kind of a disclaimer or some of the pictures or health issues pictures on the packaging of vaping product will help people to quit vaping?

Sorry, what sort of pictures would you be imagining? Like on a cigarette package, they've always got some kind of a health disclaimer there saying, "Smoking will cause cancer," and then they have some kind of graphics to tell people what will the lungs look like. Do you think it would be a good same approach on vape product as well, to put that kind of pictures on those vaping product?

So the picture would have to relate to what harm vaping is causing. Correct, like popcorn lung and all this other stuff. So at this stage, there isn't the evidence to demonstrate what that harm looks like, and I think to clarify, at no point has anybody said vaping is good for you. But what we are focused on is ensuring that vaping is made available to those who smoke to enable them to quit smoking on a least harmful product basis.

So- So the deterrent away from vaping is a separate scenario. Well, I mean, there's an argument whether vaping cause health problem or not, but I think would we think it would be good if we just put another extra sentence there that vaping may cause some kind of health issues to remind people there is a risk?

The very clear message we have repeated and consistently repeat, that is if you don't smoke, don't vape. Yes. If you smoke, vaping may assist you. The confusion around the messaging around vaping harm has resulted in people now taking up smoking in order to quit vaping, and that is the confused message suggesting that there is significant harm from vaping, when we know smoking is the product that is causing people to die. And so therefore, that is the messaging we want to do and overcomplication the messaging around vaping.

In future states, once we have our smoke-free generation, there may be consideration in that space, but it's not something we're looking at, at the moment. Supple- Yeah. Thank you, Mr. Chair. My supplementary is for the Director General. What is the Ministry's view of the emerging evidence of vaping harms that have come through in the last two years?

So the Ministry's view would be there is emerging evidence, but that the emerging evidence still supports the fact that smokers should switch to vaping. And which harms are you referring to in the emerging evidence? So there is emerging evidence around damage to lungs, and- Cancer?

And emerging, but the evidence still supports that smoking is worse for you. And is it totally proven that nicotine-containing vapes are addictive? Nicotine is an addictive substance. Riana. I just wanted to touch on some of what's already been discussed, but we know, and there's been statements made of over 200,000 people have quit each year sort of thing. Do we have specific numbers of those that are attributed to vaping,

in terms of data that might come out of either the Ministry or Te Whatu Ora? The only data we have in terms of the graphics, which I have shared quite extensively, is around that we saw the smoking rates declining up until 2018 at a relatively slow rate. And then between 2018 and 2023, we saw a massive decline, and that the only difference in that space was that vaping became available. So we did see, and it has been public health policy since 2018 that vaping is a product that has been used to help people quit smoking.

Just wanting to ask about workforce in terms of our enforcement officers. Has there been an increase in the workforce across the motu in terms of what we're doing to ensure that our vendors are adhering to law and legislation? Yeah, really encouraging. There has been an increase over the two years. So since the beginning of 2024, we've grown the team. So we are at full strength in the compliance space.

But importantly, there has been really good reporting around significant increase, and I can get those details to you around the infringement and enforcement activity that has happened. And also the compliance, assessing the compliance for most retailers has been quite high.

I think from memory it was in that 90% range of most of the retailers are compliant. But that is again, the work that we've done to set up using the compliance team as well as customs and police to actually target the illicit tobacco market as also part of that program of work.

All right. I'm going to one more sub from Carlos- Thank you, Minister... then I'll go to Janice primary. I remember last year when we are sitting here talking about vape, and we just recently passed the legislation, and you mentioned that it was only the first stage to target vaping. So how does it look like on the second stage, or what is the area we got to target on the second stage?

So I think there's a range of things that we've got to target in that space as the total environment at the moment. We have probably more regulatory controls on vapes than we do over tobacco. So there is some work in that space. The other component is actually having a regulatory regime that is consistent.

Sir. So there is a lot of work we can do in that space. So I am bringing a cabinet paper forward in the coming weeks- Yeah... to look at that other stage of the regulatory regime. So very briefly if I can, Mr. Chair. Go on. Minister, can you confirm that what you're signaling there is a direction to liberalize vapes?

No, absolutely not. What I'm signaling there is the ability to have some consistency around the level of control. We have greater visibility over the retail of vapes than we do over tobacco, and I So you're going to increase level of... Which one is it? Increase the level of control over tobacco or reduce? Yes, over tobacco. Okay. Primary, Jenny Marcroft.

Thank you very much. Switching topics now, Minister. Looking at aged care and the announcement today of, I think, $79 million. Can you let the committee know how investment into aged care will free up hospital beds, and what will this mean for those patients that get stuck on a Friday and can't get returned to residential care?

Yeah. So part of the contract, on top of a contract uplift, is actually to do some of that funding towards the resourcing to allow aged care to be funded to have weekend admissions, Friday nights, over the weekend, because there is a different type of personnel that is needed. So listening to the sector and through their contract negotiations, that has been part of the consideration around ensuring that there is workforce investment, capacity investment from that space. And it is just one portion. As you're aware, we're imminently going to receive the Ministerial Advisory Group

report onto aged care in terms of system reform, but that is the first part of that. So, the contract negotiation has been really positively engaged, and I think that we've come with a good outcome around that investment and how it can support delivery of services.

Now, you mentioned your Ministerial Advisory Group report, which is due out shortly. Can you give the committee any indication of what might be in that report? Is there anything high level that you can- I've been really clear to ensure that the Ministerial Advisory Group is independent of government, so we're not directed.

The terms of reference were very detailed around the sustainability of the system, the investment, and protecting the supply of standard beds. So those are the components in it. We should have the report next month. And we will continue to engage to ensure that that is publicly available and work through making some ministerial decisions, taking a Cabinet paper to Cabinet in this term of government around some key policy decisions.

Thank you. And just final supplementary on this. Obviously, this committee was involved in a serious piece of work around our inquiry into aged care, and particularly with a focus on dementia and neurocognitive disorders. Has that report been useful for you as a minister in this area and also too for the Ministerial Advisory Group?

Yeah. I would say it was very useful, and the key recommendations were very consistent with the initial discussions with the group around what is needing to be addressed, what should be prioritized. So, it was definitely one of the foundation sort of documents that we provided for them for their reading bag, along with the aged care funding model review, which was also part of those considerations.

So, it was a very useful report, and it has been part of their considerations. Thank you. Hard working committee. Sup. Thank you. I've got a couple of sups in lieu of my primary. Minister, the aged care sector says there's no more beds today than what there was before the budget.

Mm-hmm. And they're 95% full with an estimated 12,000 beds still needed. So what from Budget '26, if anything, goes towards new capacity? So is there any new capacity, or is this just funding existing beds- Yeah. I-... to make these decisions?

Yeah, the reality is, it is about sustaining the existing beds. We know that we've had closures. We still have a lot of work to do in that space, and we recognize that's why the importance of the aged care group there is. But I think there's also opportunities we have around investing in the access to in-home care, primary care support.

So all of those components will be part of ensuring that we have a sustainable system moving forward. But no, the big focus at the moment and the investment from both last year and this year, so I think about 149 million in total over last budget and this budget, is to sustain the existing capacity.

Encouragingly, it's talking to the providers, shifting environment. So workforce isn't the primary issue now. It is maintaining the facilities and the capacity. So there is real opportunities, but encouragingly, the providers that are dealing with investment in technology and systems is also-- There is really good.

But we recognize that the level of uplift needed to grow capacity is going to be significant. Which raises two very quick questions then. So what from Budget 2026 was dedicated towards that home care service? And, in relation to the announcement this morning, do you have any idea of how many providers are actually currently able to take weekend admissions?

I don't have the specific data, but I know that the officials have been working directly with the peak body, the Aged Care Association, around that capacity and who that- Impacts. In terms of the in-home care, that contract is still under negotiation. That will be announced in due course once that contract negotiation is resolved.

Because without any specific ring-fence money for aged care workers retention pay and training and all that sort of stuff, and you say that workforce isn't necessarily the main factor at the moment, but looking at the data that we have available, it doesn't feel like there's many providers that will be in a position to be able to actually take those weekend admissions without extra capacity.

Yeah. I- Does that worry you? The conversations that I've had both with the peak body and the providers that I've met with, there is capacity to deal with it. That is why this funding has been important to them. But I'd have to come back to you with specific data around those numbers.

All right. Yeah. No, I'm good. The chair. I had one question for this. Go for it. Supplementary based on aged care. Alzheimer's New Zealand says that Budget 2026 fails to meet the growing dementia challenge, and that Dementia Mati Ware Ware Action Plan remains unfunded and unimplemented.

Is there anything specific that you'd like to say to what is a really growing concern amongst New Zealanders, not just Alzheimer's New Zealand, that we do more towards something that is only going to become a bigger concern? When is the government going to- Yeah. So as you're aware-... come up with some funding for that?

... there's I think seven programs running at the moment, the trials that are being run, so they will be reported back around that. There is generally, I think, opportunity within the ministerial advisory group to deal with some of those growing capacity around dementia facilities- Well, and the group is reporting back in a couple of weeks.

Are you expecting that we'll be part of that? Yeah, so it is part of that overall capability, and I think there is real opportunities within that space to have earlier intervention, which I think is one of the key actions there, seeking early diagnosis and early intervention. So I think the aged care system is a continuum of care across primary in-home care, other community supports.

And I think collectively we've got an opportunity to actually deliver a lot of those things, which is early diagnosis I think is a critical component. And the carer strategy also, the work that Mr. Simmons' doing around supporting carers in that space as well. So I think there's three or four programs of work that kind of link into improving the outcomes, and we will continue to engage. But specific actions, I would really want to have the ministerial advisory group come forward.

But we are looking at the capacity around the total capacity around psychogeriatric and dementia care facilities as part of that capacity growing that we need within the space. Thank you. We're out of time. Thank you very much. Appreciate everyone's efforts today.
Thank you. Thank you. Committee, we are suspended till 3:00. Great.
That's it. Thank you. Huh? At 3:00. Yeah, 3:00. Just want to say, we've got-
